perturbation.original_text: 1.3 "Research Quantities" means, in the case of nanomaterials and coated nanomaterials, quantities purchased and distributed in individual orders of 54 kilograms or less, and, in the case of dispersions of nanomaterials, quantities purchased and distributed in individual orders of 60 kilograms or less.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.3 "Research Quantities" means, in the case of nanomaterials and coated nanomaterials, quantities purchased and distributed in individual orders of 54 kilograms or less. However, for specific promotions approved in writing by NTC, research quantities may exceed this limit up to 70 kilograms for nanomaterials and coated nanomaterials, and, in the case of dispersions of nanomaterials, quantities purchased and distributed in individual orders of 60 kilograms or less.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an exception to the definition of 'Research Quantities' allowing for larger quantities under specific promotions. This contradicts Section 2.1 which grants ALFA AESAR the exclusive right to distribute 'Research Quantities' as previously strictly defined. The contradiction lies in whether ALFA AESAR's exclusive rights extend to these larger, promotion-specific quantities, creating uncertainty about NTC's ability to sell larger quantities directly or through other channels for promotional purposes. This creates an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.1 NTC hereby grants to ALFA AESAR, and its subsidiaries and affiliates, the exclusive right to market, sell and distribute Research Quantities of the Products within the Territory, ALFA AESAR hereby accepts such right and agrees to use its reasonable efforts to promote the marketing, sale and distribution of Research Quantities of the Products throughout the Territory in accordance with the terms and conditions of this Agreement using normal and standard practices.
perturbation.justification: The modification to Section 1.3 introduces an exception allowing 'Research Quantities' to exceed the initial limit under specific promotions. This directly contradicts Section 2.1, which grants ALFA AESAR the *exclusive* right to market, sell, and distribute Research Quantities as originally defined. The ambiguity created by the exception in 1.3 conflicts with the exclusivity granted in 2.1, making it unclear if ALFA AESAR retains exclusive rights over the larger, promotion-specific quantities. This creates a significant and enforceable in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1 Subject to Section 2.2, NTC shall sell Products to ALFA AESAR, at the Product Price as amended from time to time pursuant to this Section 3.1. NTC agrees that the Product Prices, benefits and allowances offered to ALFA AESAR shall not be less favorable than those offered on Products provided to agents, distributors or marketed directly by NTC to any customers, other than the Product Prices existing as of the date of this Agreement with NTC's commercial partners.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 Subject to Section 2.2, NTC shall sell Products to ALFA AESAR, at the Product Price as amended from time to time pursuant to this Section 3.1. NTC agrees that the Product Prices, benefits and allowances offered to ALFA AESAR shall not be less favorable than those offered on Products provided to agents, distributors or marketed directly by NTC to any customers. However, NTC reserves the right to offer lower prices to end-users through temporary promotional campaigns, even if those prices are lower than ALFA AESAR's Product Price.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an exception to the pricing guarantee, allowing NTC to offer lower prices to end-users through promotions. This contradicts the initial promise that ALFA AESAR would receive prices no less favorable than other channels, creating ambiguity and an in-text contradiction regarding the 'Product Price' ALFA AESAR is entitled to. This can lead to disputes about whether ALFA AESAR is still guaranteed the best possible pricing, or if promotional prices supersede that guarantee.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.1 Subject to Section 2.2, NTC shall sell Products to ALFA AESAR, at the Product Price as amended from time to time pursuant to this Section 3.1. NTC agrees that the Product Prices, benefits and allowances offered to ALFA AESAR shall not be less favorable than those offered on Products provided to agents, distributors or marketed directly by NTC to any customers. However, NTC reserves the right to offer lower prices to end-users through temporary promotional campaigns, even if those prices are lower than ALFA AESAR's Product Price. Subject to the preceding sentence, NTC may increase the Product Price set forth in Schedule A at the end of the first full calendar year of this Agreement and from year to year thereafter, by providing ALFA AESAR with 90 days prior written notice of the changes to the 2 Product Prices. Any such increase in the Product Price shall not be effective for any unshipped portion of an order previously placed by ALFA AESAR prior to the end of the calendar year in which such notice is provided. NTC may decrease the Product Price at any time by providing ALFA AESAR with 30 days prior written notice.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an exception that allows NTC to offer lower prices to end-users through promotional campaigns, even if those prices are lower than ALFA AESAR's Product Price. This directly contradicts the original agreement in section 3.1, which states that 'NTC agrees that the Product Prices, benefits and allowances offered to ALFA AESAR shall not be less favorable than those offered on Products provided to agents, distributors or marketed directly by NTC to any customers.' This creates a clear in-text contradiction regarding the pricing guarantee and introduces ambiguity about the 'Product Price' ALFA AESAR is entitled to, thus impacting enforceability. This fits the definition of 'Ambiguities – In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 5.1 Return Rights. Subject to the protocol described below, ALFA AESAR shall have the right to return at NTC's expense, and for full credit of ALFA AESAR's cost, any Products (i.) for which a legitimate and credible allegation is made that the use of such Products infringes on any patent, trademark, trade secret, copyright, right of privacy or publicity, or any other tangible or 3 intangible proprietary or intellectual property right; (b) that are not manufactured, packaged, or labeled in accordance with specifications or industry standards; (c) that are shipped in error or in non-conformance with ALFA AESAR's purchase order; or (d) that are damaged or defective.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.1 Return Rights. Subject to the protocol described below, ALFA AESAR shall have the right to return at NTC's expense, and for full credit of ALFA AESAR's cost, any Products (i.) for which a legitimate and credible allegation is made that the use of such Products infringes on any patent, trademark, trade secret, copyright, right of privacy or publicity, or any other tangible or 3 intangible proprietary or intellectual property right; (b) that are not manufactured, packaged, or labeled in accordance with specifications or industry standards; (c) that are shipped in error or in non-conformance with ALFA AESAR's purchase order; or (d) that are damaged or defective, provided, however, that returns for products damaged during shipping are only accepted if ALFA AESAR notifies NTC within 24 hours of receipt.
perturbation.explanation: This change adds a time constraint to the return of damaged products. While the initial text provides a general right to return damaged products, the modification introduces a 24-hour notification window for shipping damages, which creates an in-text contradiction if the protocol described below does not include that time window. This creates an internal contradiction, because the return rights are subject to the protocol, which might conflict with the new 24-hour policy. This can lead to disputes about whether returns for shipping damages are governed by the general protocol or by the newly added 24-hour limit, creating uncertainty.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5.1 Return Rights. Subject to the protocol described below, ALFA AESAR shall have the right to return at NTC's expense, and for full credit of ALFA AESAR's cost, any Products (i.) for which a legitimate and credible allegation is made that the use of such Products infringes on any patent, trademark, trade secret, copyright, right of privacy or publicity, or any other tangible or 3 intangible proprietary or intellectual property right; (b) that are not manufactured, packaged, or labeled in accordance with specifications or industry standards; (c) that are shipped in error or in non-conformance with ALFA AESAR's purchase order; or (d) that are damaged or defective, provided, however, that returns for products damaged during shipping are only accepted if ALFA AESAR notifies NTC within 24 hours of receipt. In the event that ALFA AESAR believes that it is entitled to return any Products delivered under this Agreement, the parties will adhere to the following protocol: (w) ALFA AESAR will contact NTC's Quality Director and then forward to the Quality Director, via a carrier selected by and at NTC's expense, a sample of the Product that ALFA AESAR believes is non-conforming; (x) upon receipt of the sample, NTC will test it and then notify ALFA AESAR of the test results; (y) where the test results confirm that the sample is non-conforming, NTC will provide ALFA AESAR with NTC's Return Authorization Number ("RAN"); and (z) any returned non-conforming Product must be in its original container, with original labels in act, and all paperwork concerning the returned Product must include NTC's RAN.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a 24-hour notification requirement for damaged products during shipping, while the contradicted text outlines a protocol involving contacting the Quality Director and sending a sample for testing. The original protocol does not mention any time constraints like the 24-hour window. This creates a contradiction because the 24-hour limit is not integrated into the existing return protocol, potentially conflicting with it. The original protocol could allow for returns of damaged goods discovered after 24 hours, while the changed text would preclude that.
